The most widely used forms of renewable energy are biomass, hydropower, wind energy, solar energy and geothermal energy.
In order to combat global warming, lower CO2 emissions, including from electricity production, have become increasingly important.
Replacing fossil energy sources with renewable energy, i.e. the energy transition, is naturally given a lot of attention.

Leading in this, the "no serious harm" principle (Do No Significant Harm - DNSH) has been considered on the basis of six possible environmental impacts, including impacts on climate change and biodiversity.
In the context of the sustainability of energy supply, renewable energy sources and other energy sources or measures are included.
In view of climate change, it is important that in the long term reserve capacity is made up of renewable energy sources as much as possible.
